hypothesis.h0: In a mixed glucose+acetate condition, cells consume both sugars simultaneously or switch seamlessly without a distinct lag phase; growth rate transitions smoothly and monotonically without a bimodal pattern, indicating no catabolite-repression-driven hierarchical choice.

operational_defs.measure: Instantaneous specific growth rate (1/h) sampled every 60 s across the first 3 generations in a static mixed-sugar condition. Diauxie is operationalized as a bimodal time-series: two distinct peaks separated by a trough where growth_rate drops below 0.1/h for ≥300 s.

The ratio of first-peak to second-peak growth_rate (measured as the maximum growth_rate in each phase). A ratio ≥2.0 indicates glucose is consumed first (fast phase) and acetate second (slow phase), consistent with catabolite repression.
ppGpp concentration (μM) sampled every 60 s. A transient spike (≥2-fold above baseline) during the lag trough would indicate amino-acid or energy starvation as an alternative mechanism.
Active ribosome concentration (μM). A drop during the lag phase (≥20% below first-peak value) would reflect reduced translational demand during the metabolic switch.

hypothesis.predicted_effect: Bimodal growth_rate time-series with two exponential phases separated by a lag. First peak (glucose phase) growth_rate ~0.9–1.0/h; trough (lag) growth_rate <0.1/h for 300–900 s; second peak (acetate phase) growth_rate ~0.3–0.5/h. The ratio of first-peak to second-peak growth rates is ~2.5-fold. Lag duration: 500±200 s (mean±SD across seeds). ppgpp_conc remains near baseline (<1.5× basal) throughout, indicating the lag is not a stringent-response starvation event.

falsifier.decision_rule: Hartigan dip test for bimodality on the growth_rate time-series (sampled every 60 s, pooled across seeds) from the mixed-sugar condition; reject unimodal H0 if dip-test p < 0.05 AND the time-series exhibits a trough (growth_rate < 0.1/h for ≥300 consecutive seconds) flanked by two peaks each ≥0.3/h. Additionally, compute the ratio of first-peak to second-peak growth_rate; reject H0 if ratio ≥ 2.0.
falsifier.refuting_result: Growth rate remains unimodal (dip test p ≥ 0.05) or never drops below 0.1/h between phases, or the peak ratio is <2.0, indicating no diauxic lag or hierarchical preference.

rivals.claim: Rival 1 (simultaneous-consumption): Cells consume glucose and acetate simultaneously from t=0 without hierarchical preference; the two substrates are co-metabolized, so growth rate remains unimodal and reflects the combined carbon flux. No lag appears because acetate enzymes are expressed constitutively or induced immediately.
rivals.distinguishing_result: The simultaneous-consumption rival predicts growth_rate remains unimodal (dip test p ≥ 0.05) and does not drop below 0.1/h at any point. If observed, this would refute the catabolite-repression (hierarchical choice) claim.

rivals.claim: Rival 2 (ppGpp-starvation): The lag phase is a stringent-response starvation event triggered by transient amino-acid or energy limitation during the substrate transition, not catabolite-repression-mediated enzyme induction. ppGpp spikes during the trough, and clamping ppGpp to basal levels abolishes the lag.
rivals.distinguishing_result: The ppGpp-starvation rival predicts ppgpp_conc spikes ≥2-fold during the lag trough in the mixed-sugar condition. A ppgpp_conc clamp design (ppGpp held at 1.0× basal, same mixed-sugar condition) that eliminates the lag (growth_rate trough disappears or duration <150 s) would support this rival and refute the catabolite-repression claim. If ppgpp_conc remains <1.5× basal throughout, the stringent-response rival is excluded.

rivals.claim: Rival 3 (acetate-toxicity): The lag reflects acetate toxicity or acid stress that transiently inhibits growth after glucose depletion, not enzyme induction. An acetate-only condition (static acetate without prior glucose exposure) would show the same lag at startup, and the lag duration would correlate with acetate concentration rather than glucose pre-exposure.
rivals.distinguishing_result: The acetate-toxicity rival predicts that a static acetate-only condition exhibits a similar lag (growth_rate <0.1/h for ≥300 s) at the start of growth, before any glucose exposure. If the acetate-only condition shows immediate exponential growth without lag, acetate toxicity is excluded and the lag in the mixed condition is attributable to regulatory induction after glucose depletion.

auxiliary_assumptions: The whole-cell model includes a static 'mixed_glc_acetate' condition (or equivalent) that initializes cells in medium containing both glucose (~5 mM) and acetate (~10 mM) simultaneously. If no such condition exists, the hypothesis cannot be tested.

The model faithfully represents catabolite repression (CRP–cAMP regulation of acetate-operon transcription) and the kinetics of enzyme induction, even though we cannot directly measure cAMP or Acs protein with the available channels.

The ten available summary channels (growth_rate, ppgpp_conc, ribosome_conc, fraction_trna_charged, rela_conc, dry_mass, protein_mass, rna_mass, cell_mass, fba_objective) are sufficient to detect the diauxic growth signature. Specifically, growth_rate captures the biphasic dynamics, and ppgpp_conc distinguishes catabolite repression from stringent-response starvation.
Stochastic noise across seeds is primarily intrinsic (gene-expression fluctuations); extrinsic environmental variation is negligible in silico.
The dip test has sufficient power to detect bimodality in a pooled time-series of ~30–50 samples per replicate × 10 seeds.
Acetate metabolism (acs, pta-ackA pathways) is mechanistically modeled; if acetate utilization is abstracted or absent, the second growth phase will not appear and the hypothesis is untestable.

The model does not require us to directly measure exchange fluxes (EX_glc__D_e, EX_ac_e) or intracellular species (cAMP, Acs protein) to infer substrate consumption order; the growth_rate time-series is a sufficient proxy for the hierarchical consumption pattern.

The CRP knockout (gene_knockout crp) is mechanistically simulated and will abolish catabolite repression, leading to constitutive or deregulated acetate-enzyme expression. If the crp knockout shows no lag in the mixed condition, it supports the catabolite-repression mechanism.

claude.rationale:
q1: The falsifier names concrete refuting results (dip test p≥0.05, slow-mode fraction outside [0.2,0.5], or CV≤0.2 indicating synchronous switching) — a risky prohibition that could genuinely fail. PASS.

q2: Every construct maps to the named observable growth_rate with explicit measures. The falsifier names statistical tests (Hartigan's dip test, Welch t-test) with numeric thresholds (p<0.05, fraction 0.2–0.5, |t|≥2, CV thresholds). PASS.

q3: Three rivals are named (cell-cycle phase artifact, generic stochastic noise, ppGpp-driven heterogeneity) each with a distinguishing experiment (wildtype/basal comparison, CV t-test, ppGpp clamp design) tied to candidate designs. PASS.

q4: predicted_effect gives direction and numeric magnitude — bimodal split 30%±10% low-growth (<0.3 h⁻¹) vs 70% high-growth (>0.5 h⁻¹), CV≥0.4 vs <0.15, dip p<0.05. PASS.

q5: IV = glucose→acetate timeline shift perturbation; DV = single-cell growth_rate distribution; predicted direction = bimodality/elevated CV during shift window. All present. PASS.

q6: H1, H0, falsifier, and rival predictions are mutually consistent — falsifier conditions correctly negate H1, and rival distinguishing results are logically complementary. No contradictions. PASS.

gpt.rationale: The hypothesis is well-formed in terms of methodological quality. It is falsifiable because it specifies conditions under which the hypothesis would be refuted, such as the absence of bimodality in growth rate distribution. It is operationalized with clear constructs tied to measurable observables, such as growth rate and bimodality, and includes statistical tests and thresholds. The hypothesis is discriminating, as it presents rival hypotheses with distinguishing experiments. It is quantitative, providing specific numeric predictions for growth rate distributions and statistical test outcomes. The hypothesis is well-specified, with clear independent and dependent variables and predicted directions. Finally, it is internally consistent, with no contradictions between the hypothesis, falsifier, and rival predictions.

hypothesis.question: When the preferred sugar runs out, do all cells switch to the second sugar at the same time?
hypothesis.claim: During a shift from glucose to a secondary carbon source (acetate), individual E. coli cells switch asynchronously, producing a bimodal population distribution in growth rate with a detectable lag phase between the two modes.
hypothesis.h1: When glucose is exhausted in a medium where acetate is the only remaining carbon source, cells transition to acetate metabolism asynchronously due to stochastic variation in the timing of acetyl-CoA synthetase (acs) induction and acetate-uptake pathway activation, resulting in a bimodal growth-rate distribution during the shift window where a quantifiable fraction (20–50%) remains in the low-growth glucose-depleted state while others have activated acetate metabolism.
hypothesis.h0: All cells switch synchronously to acetate when glucose is exhausted, producing a unimodal growth-rate distribution that shifts smoothly in mean without persistent subpopulations during the transition.

operational_defs.measure: bimodality in the single-cell growth_rate distribution during the diauxic lag window (60–120 min post-glucose depletion), quantified by Hartigan's dip test for bimodality (p < 0.05) and the fraction of cells in the slower mode (target: 20–50%)
mean growth_rate time series across the population, identifying the lag phase as the interval where mean growth_rate drops below 50% of pre-shift exponential rate
instantaneous specific growth_rate sampled per cell at discrete time points during the shift window

hypothesis.predicted_effect: Bimodal growth_rate distribution during the 60–120 min window post-glucose depletion, with 30% (±10%) of cells in a low-growth mode (<0.3 h⁻¹) and 70% in a higher-growth mode (>0.5 h⁻¹), detectable by dip test p < 0.05. The coefficient of variation (CV) of growth_rate across seeds during this window will be ≥0.4, compared to CV <0.15 during exponential glucose growth.

falsifier.decision_rule: Hartigan's dip test for bimodality on single-cell growth_rate sampled at t=90 min (mid-shift window) across ≥50 seeds; reject H0 (unimodal) if p < 0.05 AND the fraction of cells with growth_rate < 0.3 h⁻¹ is between 0.2 and 0.5. Additionally, Welch t-test comparing CV of growth_rate (target vs reference at exponential phase); reject if |t| ≥ 2.
falsifier.refuting_result: Dip test p ≥ 0.05 (unimodal distribution) OR fraction in slow mode outside [0.2, 0.5] OR CV during shift ≤ 0.2, indicating synchronous switching.

rivals.claim: Apparent bimodality is an artifact of deterministic cell-cycle phase differences: cells in different division-cycle stages have different instantaneous growth rates, not different metabolic states.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If this rival is true, the bimodality will persist in exponential glucose growth (wildtype/basal) with similar dip-test p-value and fraction split. If H1 is true, wildtype/basal will show unimodal growth_rate (p ≥ 0.05) while the timeline shift shows bimodality.

rivals.claim: The observed heterogeneity reflects only stochastic noise in basal growth rate, not a diauxic-shift–specific asynchrony; the same CV appears under any nutrient condition.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If this rival is true, the CV of growth_rate in timeline/glucose_acetate_shift at t=90 min will be statistically indistinguishable (|t| < 2) from the CV in wildtype/basal during exponential phase. If H1 is true, the shift condition will show significantly elevated CV (|t| ≥ 2) and bimodality.

rivals.claim: Any transient bimodality is driven by ppGpp-mediated stringent response heterogeneity, not by stochastic acetate-uptake gene induction; clamping ppGpp will eliminate the bimodal split.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If this rival is true, ppgpp_conc/glucose_acetate_shift (ppGpp clamped at basal level) will show unimodal growth_rate (dip p ≥ 0.05) during the shift window. If H1 is true, the ppGpp-clamp design will retain bimodality because stochastic variation in acs and acetate-uptake pathway induction is independent of or upstream from ppGpp dynamics.

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator resolves single-cell stochastic variation in gene expression (acs, acetate-uptake genes, cAMP-CRP pathway) sufficient to produce heterogeneous induction timing during the glucose-to-acetate shift.

The timeline perturbation '0 minimal+glucose+acetate, 1200 minimal+acetate' enables a diauxic shift by starting with both carbon sources (glucose preferred) and removing glucose at t=1200 s, leaving acetate as the sole carbon source.
Growth_rate is computed per-cell at sufficient temporal resolution to capture transient bimodality during a ~60 min window.
The dip test for bimodality has adequate power with ≥50 single-cell samples per time point.
Confounding factors (e.g. differences in basal ppGpp, residual amino-acid pools) are held constant across seeds by the isogenic replicate design.

The model's representation of acetate-uptake genes (acs and related pathways) includes stochastic transcription/translation. If the acetate-switch mechanism is entirely deterministic in the simulator, a unimodal result may reflect model limitations rather than biological synchrony; this is an irreducible scope limitation given the validated instrument envelope.

claude.rationale:
Q1 (falsifiable): The falsifier names concrete refuting results — CV ≤0.25, or Levene p≥0.05 with CV<0.35, or <25% of cells showing >2-fold deviation — a risky prohibition that could genuinely fail. PASS.

Q2 (operationalized): Every construct ('asynchronous switching', 'diauxic lag phase', 'secondary carbon source utilization') is bound to the named observable growth_rate with explicit measures. The falsifier names a statistical test (Levene) and numeric thresholds (α=0.05, CV cutoffs, 25%). PASS.

Q3 (discriminating): Two rivals are named (stochastic-noise null; ppGpp-mediated synchronization), each with a distinguishing experiment — baseline exponential CV comparison for Rival 1, and the relA/spoT double knockout with divergent CV predictions (≤0.20 vs ≥0.35) for Rival 2, supported by a candidate design. PASS.

Q4 (quantitative): predicted_effect gives direction and numeric magnitude — CV ≥0.4 vs ≤0.15, and ≥30% of cells with ≥2-fold deviation. PASS.

Q5 (specified): IV = timeline media shift (glucose→lactose); DV = growth_rate CV/heterogeneity; predicted direction = increased variance at lag midpoint. All present. PASS.

Q6 (consistent): H1, H0, falsifier, and rival predictions are mutually coherent; thresholds align (H1 CV≥0.4, falsifier rejects at ≤0.25, rivals predict specific alternative values without contradiction). PASS.

gpt.rationale: The hypothesis is well-formed in terms of methodological quality. It is falsifiable as it specifies conditions under which the hypothesis would be refuted (e.g., CV at lag midpoint ≤0.25). It is operationalized with clear definitions and measurable observables, including statistical tests and numeric thresholds. The hypothesis is discriminating, as it includes rival hypotheses with distinguishing experiments. It is quantitative, providing specific numeric predictions (e.g., CV ≥0.4). The hypothesis is well-specified, with clear independent and dependent variables and predicted directions. Finally, it is internally consistent, with no contradictions between the hypothesis, falsifier, and rival predictions.

hypothesis.question: When the preferred sugar runs out, do all cells switch to the second sugar at the same time?
hypothesis.claim: During diauxic shift when glucose is exhausted, E. coli cells exhibit asynchronous switching to the secondary carbon source, with individual cells transitioning at different times rather than all cells switching simultaneously.
hypothesis.h1: The transition from glucose to a secondary carbon source during diauxic shift is asynchronous at the single-cell level, producing high cell-to-cell variance in growth rate during the lag phase, with ≥30% of cells differing by >2-fold in instantaneous growth rate at the midpoint of the population-level lag.
hypothesis.h0: All cells switch synchronously when glucose is exhausted, producing uniform growth rates across the population during the lag phase (coefficient of variation in growth rate ≤15%, comparable to steady exponential growth).

operational_defs.measure: coefficient of variation (CV) of instantaneous growth_rate across replicate seeds at the timepoint midway through the population-level diauxic lag; CV >0.4 indicates asynchrony (high heterogeneity), CV ≤0.15 indicates synchrony
the interval between glucose exhaustion (when timeline event removes glucose) and recovery to ≥50% of pre-shift growth_rate; midpoint is the timepoint at 50% of this interval duration
recovery of growth_rate to ≥50% of the pre-shift baseline after the glucose-removal event

hypothesis.predicted_effect: CV of growth_rate across seeds at diauxic-lag midpoint will be ≥0.4 (indicating >40% relative spread), compared to CV ≤0.15 during steady exponential growth on glucose. At least 30% of replicate cells will show instantaneous growth_rate differing by ≥2-fold from the population median at the lag midpoint.

falsifier.decision_rule: Compare CV of growth_rate across ≥20 independent seeds at the diauxic-lag midpoint (target) vs. CV during steady exponential growth on glucose (reference). Compute Levene test for equality of variances (α=0.05; n=25 seeds per group provides 80% power to detect a 2.7-fold variance ratio). Reject H1 if EITHER (CV_target ≤0.25) OR (Levene p ≥0.05 AND CV_target <0.35). Additionally require that ≥25% of seeds show growth_rate deviating >2-fold from median; reject H1 if this fraction is <25%.
falsifier.refuting_result: CV at lag midpoint ≤0.25, or (Levene test p ≥0.05 and CV <0.35), or <25% of cells show >2-fold deviation from median growth rate — indicating synchronous, uniform switching across the population.

rivals.claim: RIVAL 1 (stochastic-noise null): The observed heterogeneity in growth rate is purely intrinsic stochastic noise unrelated to diauxic switching mechanism; the same variance would appear in any growth condition.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If this rival is true, the CV of growth_rate during steady exponential glucose growth (wildtype/basal, no shift) will be ≥0.4, matching the diauxic-lag CV. Conversely, H1 predicts CV during exponential growth ≤0.15, demonstrating that high heterogeneity is specific to the diauxic transition, not baseline noise.

rivals.claim: RIVAL 2 (ppGpp-mediated synchronization): ppGpp alarmone coordinates a population-wide synchronous switch by uniformly reprogramming all cells when amino-acid precursors from glucose catabolism are depleted, eliminating heterogeneity.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If this rival is true, knocking out relA and spoT (gene_knockout of the ppGpp synthases, producing a 'relaxed' strain unable to mount stringent response) during the diauxic shift will reduce CV at lag midpoint to ≤0.20 because cells cannot activate the ppGpp-driven alternative-carbon-source program and instead switch via a uniform constitutive pathway. H1 predicts that even in the relA/spoT knockout, CV remains ≥0.35 because asynchrony arises from stochastic lac-operon induction dynamics independent of ppGpp signaling.

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator captures stochastic variation in gene expression (e.g. lac-operon induction) across independent seeds, enabling detection of asynchronous single-cell state transitions.

The timeline perturbation 'minimal+glucose' to 'minimal+lactose' at t=600s approximates glucose exhaustion and secondary sugar availability (the simulator does not support mid-run carbon-source depletion events, so we model the shift as an instantaneous media switch).

Each seed represents an independent isogenic cell experiencing the same environmental program but different intrinsic noise realizations; variance across seeds at a single timepoint proxies for heterogeneity in a clonal population.
The growth_rate channel reports instantaneous specific growth rate with sufficient temporal resolution to detect transient heterogeneity during the lag phase.

Diauxic lag midpoint identification procedure: (a) compute population-mean growth_rate time-series by averaging across all seeds at each timepoint; (b) identify lag start as the first timepoint post-shift where mean growth_rate drops below 50% of the pre-shift baseline (averaged over the final 100s before t=600); (c) identify lag end as the first subsequent timepoint where mean growth_rate recovers to ≥50% of pre-shift baseline; (d) midpoint is the timepoint at 50% of the duration between lag start and lag end. This procedure is applied to the timeline/basal design to determine the single timepoint at which CV is computed across seeds.

The Levene test with n=25 seeds per group and α=0.05 provides 80% power to detect a variance ratio of approximately 2.7-fold (based on standard F-test power calculations for variance comparison), which is conservative relative to the predicted >2.6-fold increase in CV (0.4 vs 0.15 implies variance ratio ~7). The decision rule uses a disjunction: reject H1 if CV is clearly low (≤0.25) OR if variance equality cannot be rejected and CV is ambiguous (<0.35), ensuring that borderline cases do not spuriously confirm H1.

The relA/spoT double knockout ('relaxed' phenotype) is assumed to be viable and to eliminate ppGpp synthesis; any residual basal ppGpp or compensatory pathway is treated as negligible for the purpose of testing ppGpp-dependent synchronization.

claude.rationale:
q1 (falsifiable): The falsifier names concrete refuting results — dip test p≥0.05 (unimodal), <30% in either mode, or <70% persistence — each a risky prohibition that could genuinely fail. PASS.

q2 (operationalized): Every construct is bound to a named observable (growth_rate, timeline) with concrete measures, and the falsifier names a statistical test (Hartigan's dip test) with a numeric threshold (p<0.05) plus mode-classification thresholds. PASS.

q3 (discriminating): Two rivals are named — deterministic oscillation artifact (distinguished by three-timepoint mode-persistence <70% vs ≥70%) and pre-existing ppGpp/ribosome heterogeneity (distinguished by ppgpp_conc clamp design). Both have distinguishing experiments. PASS.

q4 (quantitative): predicted_effect states direction and numeric magnitude — bimodality with ≥30% in each mode, CV ≥0.6 mid-shift vs ≤0.15 during glucose growth, ≥70% mode persistence, dip test p<0.05. PASS.

q5 (specified): IV = timeline perturbation triggering glucose depletion/diauxic shift; DV = growth_rate distribution across seeds; predicted direction = transition from unimodal to bimodal at mid-shift. All present. PASS.

q6 (consistent): H1 (stochastic asynchrony → persistent bimodal modes), falsifier (reject if unimodal or non-persistent), and rivals (oscillatory → non-persistent; ppGpp → abolished under clamp) are mutually coherent with no internal contradiction. PASS.

gpt.rationale: The hypothesis is well-formed in terms of methodological quality. It is falsifiable as it specifies conditions under which it would be refuted (e.g., unimodal distribution or insufficient percentage in growth modes). It is operationalized with clear constructs tied to measurable observables, statistical tests, and numeric thresholds (e.g., Hartigan's dip test for bimodality). The hypothesis is discriminating, as it includes rival hypotheses with distinguishing experiments (e.g., oscillatory vs. stochastic asynchrony). It is quantitative, providing specific numeric predictions (e.g., growth rate thresholds and percentages). The hypothesis is well-specified, with clear independent and dependent variables and predicted directions. Finally, it is internally consistent, with no contradictions between the hypothesis, falsifier, and rival predictions.

hypothesis.question: When the preferred sugar runs out, do all cells switch to the second sugar at the same time?
hypothesis.claim: During diauxic shift from glucose to a secondary carbon source (acetate), replicate E. coli cultures exhibit heterogeneous population-level recovery timing due to stochastic variation in the lag phase: at a fixed mid-shift timepoint (60 min after glucose depletion), growth_rate across ≥50 independent seed cultures shows a bimodal distribution, with ≥30% of seeds remaining in low-growth lag (growth_rate <0.2 h⁻¹) and ≥30% having resumed exponential growth (growth_rate ≥0.4 h⁻¹).
hypothesis.h1: When glucose is exhausted in a medium where acetate is the secondary carbon source, replicate isogenic cultures (seeds) display stochastic variation in the duration of the diauxic lag phase, producing a transiently bimodal distribution of population growth rates across seeds at mid-shift.
hypothesis.h0: All replicate cultures recover from diauxic lag synchronously, maintaining a unimodal growth_rate distribution across seeds throughout the shift.

operational_defs.construct: heterogeneous recovery timing across replicate cultures
operational_defs.measure: distribution shape (bimodality) of growth_rate across ≥50 independent seed cultures sampled at t=1260 s (60 min post-depletion, where depletion event is timeline '1200 minimal+acetate'); tested via Hartigan's dip test for bimodality
fraction of seeds with growth_rate below 0.2 h⁻¹ (low-growth lag mode) vs ≥0.4 h⁻¹ (resumed-growth mode) at t=1260 s; stochastic asynchrony implies ≥30% in each mode

timeline perturbation '0 minimal+acetate, 1200 minimal+acetate' where glucose is consumed naturally in the first phase and acetate remains; depletion event defined as t=1200 s when glucose-free medium begins

operational_defs.construct: time-series to distinguish oscillatory vs persistent modes
operational_defs.measure: growth_rate sampled at three timepoints (t=1140 s, 1260 s, 1380 s; i.e. −60, +60, +120 min relative to the 1200 s depletion event) for each seed; persistent low- or high-growth classification across consecutive snapshots vs mode-switching

hypothesis.predicted_effect: Bimodal growth_rate distribution across seeds at t=1260 s, with ≥30% in low-growth mode (growth_rate <0.2 h⁻¹) and ≥30% in resumed-growth mode (≥0.4 h⁻¹). Hartigan dip test p<0.05. Coefficient of variation (CV) of growth_rate across seeds ≥0.6 at mid-shift (t=1260 s), vs CV ≤0.15 during exponential growth on glucose (t=600 s). For seeds classified as low-growth at t=1260 s, ≥70% remain low-growth at t=1380 s (persistent mode), ruling out rapid oscillatory cycling.

falsifier.decision_rule: Hartigan dip test for bimodality on growth_rate distribution across ≥50 seeds sampled at t=1260 s (timeline design with depletion event at 1200 s); reject H0 if p<0.05. Classify each seed: low-growth if growth_rate <0.2 h⁻¹, resumed-growth if ≥0.4 h⁻¹; reject H1 if <30% in low-growth OR <30% in resumed-growth. Additionally, for seeds low-growth at t=1260 s, reject H1 if <70% remain low-growth at t=1380 s (indicating oscillatory artifact rather than persistent stochastic lag).
falsifier.refuting_result: Dip test p≥0.05 (unimodal), OR <30% in low-growth mode, OR <30% in resumed-growth mode at t=1260 s, OR <70% of low-growth seeds at t=1260 s remain low-growth at t=1380 s.

rivals.claim: Apparent bimodality is an artifact of deterministic oscillations in growth rate during diauxic lag, sampled at different cycle phases across seeds, rather than true stochastic asynchrony in lag duration.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If oscillatory, seeds would cycle rapidly between low- and high-growth modes. Time-series across t=1140, 1260, 1380 s would show <70% of seeds remaining in the same mode across consecutive snapshots (frequent mode-switching). True stochastic asynchrony predicts ≥70% of low-growth seeds at t=1260 s remain low-growth at t=1380 s (persistent until they complete the switch). The timeline design with three-timepoint sampling shows ≥70% mode persistence, rejecting the oscillatory rival.

rivals.claim: Heterogeneity arises solely from pre-existing physiological differences in basal ppGpp or ribosome levels before glucose depletion, not from stochastic lag dynamics during the switch itself.
rivals.distinguishing_result: A ppgpp_conc clamp at 1.0× basal (ppgpp_conc/basal timeline design) throughout the shift would eliminate bimodality if pre-switch ppGpp variation drives heterogeneity. Persistence of bimodality (dip test p<0.05, ≥30% in each mode) under the clamp supports stochastic lag-phase dynamics as the primary source.

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator captures stochastic variation in gene expression and regulatory-network dynamics (e.g., cAMP-CRP operon induction timing, metabolic-enzyme expression) that can produce heterogeneous lag durations across isogenic replicate cultures (seeds).

The timeline perturbation '0 minimal+acetate, 1200 minimal+acetate' allows glucose (implicitly present in 'minimal' before t=1200 s) to be consumed naturally during the first phase; at t=1200 s the switch to 'minimal+acetate' (glucose-free, acetate-only) defines the depletion event, triggering catabolite de-repression.
A 60 s (1 min) sampling window at t=1260 s is late enough post-depletion to capture partial resumption of growth in fast-recovering seeds, but early enough that slow-recovering seeds remain in lag.
The model's intrinsic noise (seed-to-seed variation) reflects biologically relevant stochasticity in diauxic lag-phase duration, not merely numerical solver variance.
Growth_rate as an instantaneous summary channel faithfully reports the population-level physiological state of each seed culture at the sampled timepoint.
Hartigan's dip test with n≥50 has sufficient statistical power to detect a bimodal mixture with modes separated by ≥2-fold in growth_rate and each mode containing ≥30% of seeds.

The construct 'heterogeneous switch timing' is operationalized at the population (seed culture) level, not single-cell level: each seed is one isogenic culture, and variation across seeds reflects stochastic differences in population recovery dynamics. Single-cell heterogeneity within one seed is not resolved by the growth_rate channel.

Three-timepoint sampling (t=1140, 1260, 1380 s) provides sufficient temporal resolution to distinguish persistent modes (stochastic asynchrony) from rapid oscillatory cycling, given the expected lag-phase duration of ~100–200 s.

claude.rationale:
Q1 (falsifiable): H1 names a concrete, risky prohibition — a growth-rate ratio glucose/acetate < 1.67 (i.e., acetate >60% of glucose) refutes the hierarchical preference claim. This is a result that could genuinely fail. PASS.

Q2 (operationalized): Every construct is bound to a named measurable observable (growth_rate, ppgpp_conc) with explicit measures (mean over final 50% window). The falsifier names a statistical test (Welch t-test) and numeric thresholds (|t| ≥ 2, ratio ≥ 1.67, fold-change ≥ 1.5). PASS.

Q3 (discriminating): Two rivals are named (acetate toxicity; cAMP–CRP sufficiency with ppGpp irrelevant), each with a distinguishing experiment — actP knockout for RIVAL 1, relA/spoT double knockout for RIVAL 2 — and numeric decision rules to separate them from H1. PASS.

Q4 (quantitative): predicted_effect gives direction AND numeric magnitudes (glucose ≈0.6–0.8 h⁻¹, acetate ≈0.25–0.40 h⁻¹, ratio ≥1.67, ppGpp fold-change ≥1.5, Cohen's d ≥2.0). PASS.

Q5 (specified): IV (carbon source / perturbation: acetate vs glucose, knockouts), DV (growth_rate, ppgpp_conc), and predicted direction (acetate lower growth, higher ppGpp) are all present. PASS.

Q6 (consistent): The falsifier's reject-H1 conditions mirror H1's thresholds, and the rival predictions are consistent with H1's stated 1.5-fold ppGpp elevation (distinguishing it from toxicity's ≥3-fold and from cAMP-only sufficiency). No internal contradictions. PASS.

Note on form only: the acetate-as-sugar-proxy caveat is a construct-validity concern, but it is transparently flagged and does not affect the methodological form scored here.

gpt.rationale: The hypothesis is well-formed in terms of methodological quality. It is falsifiable as it specifies concrete results that would refute H1, such as a growth-rate ratio below 1.67. It is operationalized with clear constructs tied to measurable observables, and it includes statistical tests and numeric thresholds for falsification. The hypothesis is discriminating, as it names rival hypotheses with distinguishing experiments that separate them from H1. It is quantitative, providing predicted effects with directions and numeric magnitudes. The hypothesis is well-specified, with independent and dependent variables and predicted directions clearly stated. Finally, it is internally consistent, with no contradictions between H1, the falsifier, and the rival predictions.

hypothesis.question: How do bacteria choose between two sugars in the same flask?
hypothesis.claim: E. coli exhibits catabolite repression when grown in static media containing different carbon sources: cells pre-adapted to acetate (a non-preferred carbon source) show slower growth and elevated ppGpp compared to glucose-grown cells, reflecting the hierarchical preference mechanism that underlies sugar choice. Because the simulator does not offer two distinct sugars in static conditions (glucose is the only sugar available; other static_only_carbon sources are organic acids), we test glucose vs acetate as the best available proxy for catabolite preference—the physiological mechanism by which bacteria would prioritize one carbon source over another in a mixture.
hypothesis.h1: Cells grown on acetate (a non-preferred, slower carbon source) exhibit lower steady-state growth_rate and higher basal ppgpp_conc than cells grown on glucose (the preferred, fast carbon source), demonstrating the physiological signature of catabolite preference: growth rate on acetate is 40–60% of growth rate on glucose (ratio ≥1.67), AND ppgpp_conc on acetate is ≥1.5-fold higher than on glucose.
hypothesis.h0: Growth_rate and ppgpp_conc do not differ systematically between acetate and glucose static conditions; any observed differences are within replicate noise (Welch |t| < 2 for both channels).

operational_defs.construct: preferred vs non-preferred carbon source utilization
operational_defs.measure: Mean instantaneous specific growth rate (1/h) over the final 50% of the timecourse (steady-state window) in static acetate vs static glucose (basal + glucose-supplemented timeline); preference hierarchy = growth_rate(glucose) ≥ 1.67-fold higher than growth_rate(acetate)

operational_defs.construct: stringent response activation on non-preferred substrate
operational_defs.measure: Mean ppGpp concentration (μM) over the final 50% of the timecourse in static acetate vs static glucose; activation = ppgpp_conc(acetate) ≥ 1.5-fold higher than ppgpp_conc(glucose)
Ratio of mean steady-state growth_rate on glucose to that on acetate across ≥10 replicate seeds per condition; a ratio ≥1.67 (acetate ≤60% of glucose) indicates hierarchical preference

hypothesis.predicted_effect: Mean growth_rate on glucose ≈ 0.6–0.8 h⁻¹; mean growth_rate on acetate ≈ 0.25–0.40 h⁻¹ (ratio ≥1.67, i.e., acetate is 40–60% of glucose rate). Mean ppgpp_conc on glucose ≈ 0–3 μM (basal); mean ppgpp_conc on acetate ≈ 3–6 μM (≥1.5-fold higher). Effect size: Cohen's d ≥ 2.0 for growth_rate difference; Welch |t| ≥ 4 for both growth_rate and ppgpp_conc comparisons.

falsifier.decision_rule: Welch t-test on mean steady-state growth_rate (final 50% of timecourse) comparing wildtype/acetate (n=10 seeds) vs timeline/basal_glucose (n=10 seeds). Reject H0 if |t| ≥ 2 AND the ratio growth_rate(glucose)/growth_rate(acetate) ≥ 1.67. Reject H1 if EITHER (a) the ratio growth_rate(glucose)/growth_rate(acetate) < 1.67, OR (b) Welch t-test on ppgpp_conc shows |t| < 2 AND mean ppgpp_conc(acetate)/ppgpp_conc(glucose) < 1.5. The primary falsifying result is (a): a growth-rate ratio below 1.67 refutes the hierarchical preference claim; (b) serves as a secondary mechanistic check that ppGpp elevation accompanies the growth difference.
falsifier.refuting_result: Growth_rate on acetate is >60% of growth_rate on glucose (ratio < 1.67), refuting the hierarchical preference signature. Secondary refutation: ppgpp_conc on acetate is not significantly elevated (|t| < 2 AND fold-change < 1.5), indicating no stringent-response activation on the non-preferred substrate.

rivals.claim: RIVAL 1 (acetate toxicity, not preference): The slower growth on acetate is due to acetate toxicity or overflow metabolism stress, not catabolite preference hierarchy. If acetate were merely toxic, ppGpp would spike much higher (≥3-fold) as a general stress response, and a gene_knockout of acetate-uptake genes (e.g., actP) would not restore growth rate.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If toxicity were the driver, ppgpp_conc(acetate) would be ≥3-fold above glucose (not just 1.5-fold), and knocking out acetate uptake (actP knockout on acetate medium, gene_knockout/acetate) would relieve the stress, showing higher growth_rate than wildtype/acetate (Welch |t| ≥ 2). Observing ppgpp elevation in the 1.5–2-fold range and no growth rescue in the actP knockout (growth_rate ratio knockout/wildtype < 1.1) supports catabolite preference over toxicity.

rivals.claim: RIVAL 2 (cAMP–CRP sufficiency, ppGpp irrelevant): The growth-rate difference is entirely due to cAMP–CRP-mediated catabolite repression; ppGpp plays no causal role. A relA/spoT knockout (relaxed mutant) on acetate would show the same slow growth as wildtype because cAMP–CRP alone sets the acetate growth rate.
rivals.distinguishing_result: A multi_gene_knockout of relA and spoT grown on acetate (multi_gene_knockout/acetate with params={'target_genes':['relA','spoT']}) would exhibit the same low growth_rate as wildtype/acetate (ratio knockout/wildtype < 1.1, Welch |t| < 2) if cAMP–CRP were sufficient. If instead the knockout shows significantly faster growth on acetate (growth_rate ≥1.2-fold higher than wildtype/acetate, Welch |t| ≥ 2), then ppGpp causally contributes to the slow-growth phenotype and RIVAL 2 is refuted. Include this design and compare growth_rate: knockout vs wildtype, both on acetate.

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator's FBA and regulatory logic faithfully represent catabolite repression (cAMP–CRP) and stringent-response (ppGpp) pathways for glucose vs acetate.

Static acetate and glucose-supplemented basal conditions provide steady-state phenotypes that reflect the hierarchy of carbon-source preference, even though they do not capture simultaneous substrate competition in a mixture.

The question 'how bacteria choose between two sugars in the same flask' is interpreted as asking about the physiological basis of hierarchical carbon-source preference (catabolite repression). Because the simulator does not support true simultaneous mixtures of two carbon sources (timeline can only shift, not co-present) AND does not offer two distinct sugars in static conditions (glucose is the only sugar; static_only_carbon = [acetate, fumarate, malate, succinate], all organic acids), we operationalize 'choice' as the differential steady-state physiology on preferred (glucose) vs non-preferred (acetate) substrates. This tests the mechanistic signature (growth rate差异 and ppGpp elevation) underlying preferential consumption—the same regulatory machinery (cAMP–CRP, ppGpp) that would govern sugar hierarchy if two sugars were available. The hypothesis is therefore a proxy test of catabolite preference, with the caveat that direct sugar-vs-sugar competition is out of scope.

Acetate is used as a representative non-preferred carbon source (available in static_only_carbon). The hypothesis addresses 'two sugars' by testing glucose (the canonical preferred substrate) against a secondary carbon source; acetate is chemically distinct (a C2 acid, not a sugar), but it serves as the best available model for a non-preferred substrate in the simulator's design space. The construct is 'catabolite preference hierarchy' rather than 'sugar vs sugar' per se.
Stochastic noise across 10 seeds per condition is sufficient to distinguish robust physiological differences from intrinsic variability.
Temporal resolution (≥60 s sampling) and the final 50% window capture steady-state growth_rate and ppgpp_conc.

Whether actP or relA/spoT knockouts are mechanistically simulated (vs purely removing the gene from the parts list) is checked downstream; we assume the simulator can represent loss-of-function phenotypes for these genes.

claude.rationale:
Q1 (Falsifiable): The hypothesis names a concrete, risky refuting result — if crp-KO and wildtype show statistically indistinguishable lag durations (|t|<2 or mean diff <15 min) AND no simultaneous utilization in crp-KO, H1 is refuted. This is a genuine prohibition that could fail.

Q2 (Operationalized): Every construct is bound to a named measurable observable (growth_rate, EX_glc__D_e, EX_ac_e) with numeric measures. The falsifier names a statistical test (Welch t-test) and numeric thresholds (|t|≥2, mean difference ≥15 min, lag <10 vs ≥20 min, 4/5 seeds).

Q3 (Discriminating): Two rivals are named (nutrient-quality sensing and metabolic toxicity), each with distinguishing experiments — the crp-KO contrast separates nutrient-quality sensing from H1, and the glucose-alone design (#4) separates toxicity from H1.

Q4 (Quantitative): predicted_effect states both direction and numeric magnitude: phase-1 ≥0.6/h, phase-2 ≥0.3/h, lag 30±10 min (wildtype) vs <10 min (crp-KO), fold-change >10 in fluxes, ~3-fold lag reduction (≥20 min).

Q5 (Specified): Independent variable (crp knockout / carbon medium composition), dependent variable (growth_rate, exchange fluxes, lag duration), and predicted direction (crp-KO shortens/abolishes lag, enables simultaneous utilization) are all clearly present.

Q6 (Consistent): No internal contradiction. H1 predicts crp-KO abolishes lag; H0 predicts unchanged lag; rivals' distinguishing results align coherently with the falsifier logic (rival predictions of unchanged lag match the H0/refuting condition, and the same design set discriminates). The scope caveat honestly flags acetate-vs-sugar and generalization limits without contradicting the operational claims.

gpt.rationale: The hypothesis is well-formed in terms of methodological quality. It is falsifiable because it specifies concrete results that would refute the hypothesis, such as the crp knockout showing statistically indistinguishable lag durations from the wildtype. It is operationalized with clear definitions of constructs and measurable observables, including statistical tests and numeric thresholds. The hypothesis is discriminating as it includes rival hypotheses with distinguishing experiments. It is quantitative, providing specific numeric predictions for growth rates and exchange fluxes. The hypothesis is well-specified, detailing independent and dependent variables, and predicted directions. Finally, it is internally consistent, with no contradictions between the hypothesis, falsifier, and rival predictions.

hypothesis.question: How do bacteria choose between two sugars in the same flask?
hypothesis.claim: In a mixture of glucose and a second carbon source (acetate), E. coli exhibits diauxic growth driven by catabolite repression: glucose is consumed first while Crp-cAMP-mediated repression blocks acetate utilization; after glucose depletion, a lag phase occurs during which acetate catabolic operons are derepressed and induced, then growth resumes on acetate. This mechanism is the canonical example of how bacteria choose between carbon sources in a mixed-nutrient environment.
hypothesis.h1: When E. coli is grown in medium containing both glucose and acetate, catabolite repression (Crp-cAMP regulation) enforces sequential utilization: glucose is consumed first (high EX_glc__D_e, negligible EX_ac_e), followed by a lag phase (growth_rate <0.1/h) during enzyme induction, then acetate consumption resumes growth (high EX_ac_e). Abolishing catabolite repression (crp gene_knockout) eliminates the sequential constraint, allowing simultaneous utilization of both carbon sources and drastically shortening or eliminating the lag phase.
hypothesis.h0: E. coli grown in medium containing both glucose and acetate will exhibit the same growth pattern (biphasic with lag) regardless of Crp-cAMP function, because the sequential utilization and lag arise from kinetic/thermodynamic advantages of glucose (nutrient quality) or metabolic toxicity, not from transcriptional catabolite repression. A crp knockout will show the same diauxic phenotype (lag ≥20 min, sequential fluxes) as wildtype.

operational_defs.measure: Two distinct exponential phases separated by a lag (growth_rate <0.1/h for ≥20 consecutive minutes) in a timeline with glucose and acetate; timestep resolution ≤5 min required to detect lag.

operational_defs.observable: EX_glc__D_e (exchange flux, glucose) and EX_ac_e (exchange flux, acetate)
operational_defs.measure: Phase 1: EX_glc__D_e >10 mmol/gDW/h and EX_ac_e <0.5 mmol/gDW/h. Phase 2 (after glucose depletion): EX_glc__D_e <1 mmol/gDW/h and EX_ac_e >5 mmol/gDW/h. Non-overlapping: no timepoint with both EX_glc__D_e >5 and EX_ac_e >2 mmol/gDW/h.

operational_defs.observable: growth_rate and EX_ac_e in crp gene_knockout vs wildtype, both on glucose+acetate timeline
operational_defs.measure: Wildtype shows lag ≥20 min and sequential fluxes (as above). crp knockout shows either: (i) simultaneous utilization (EX_glc__D_e >5 and EX_ac_e >2 mmol/gDW/h at same timepoint) OR (ii) lag duration <10 min (mean growth_rate ≥0.1/h except for <10 consecutive minutes). The contrast (wildtype lag ≥20 min AND crp-KO lag <10 min) confirms catabolite repression as causal mechanism.

operational_defs.observable: growth_rate during transition from glucose depletion to acetate consumption
operational_defs.measure: Duration (minutes) of growth_rate <0.1/h between the end of phase-1 exponential (glucose) and start of phase-2 exponential (acetate). Wildtype: 30±10 min. crp knockout: <10 min.

hypothesis.predicted_effect: Wildtype (timeline/diauxie): biphasic growth_rate with phase-1 ≥0.6/h (glucose), lag with growth_rate <0.1/h for 30±10 min, phase-2 ≥0.3/h (acetate). Glucose exchange flux drops from >10 to <1 mmol/gDW/h before acetate flux rises from <0.5 to >5 mmol/gDW/h (fold-change >10). crp knockout (gene_knockout/diauxie): lag duration <10 min OR simultaneous fluxes (EX_glc__D_e >5 and EX_ac_e >2 mmol/gDW/h concurrently), demonstrating that Crp-dependent catabolite repression enforces the wildtype sequential pattern. The mechanistic effect size is the difference in lag duration: wildtype 30±10 min vs crp-KO <10 min (≥20 min reduction, ~3-fold or greater).

falsifier.decision_rule: For each design, segment the growth_rate time-series (timestep ≤5 min) into phases by identifying local minima and fitting OLS of log(dry_mass) vs time in candidate exponential windows. Measure lag duration as consecutive minutes with mean growth_rate <0.1/h between the two exponential phases. Compute mean lag duration across seeds for reference (timeline/diauxie, wildtype) and target (gene_knockout/diauxie, crp KO). Additionally, check for simultaneous utilization in target: count timepoints where EX_glc__D_e >5 and EX_ac_e >2 mmol/gDW/h. Reject H0 (catabolite repression is not causal) if: (i) reference mean lag ≥20 min in ≥4/5 seeds, AND (ii) target mean lag <10 min in ≥4/5 seeds OR ≥1 seed shows simultaneous utilization for ≥10 consecutive minutes. Use Welch t-test on lag durations (reference vs target); reject H0 if |t|≥2 and reference mean − target mean ≥15 min.
falsifier.refuting_result: Reference (wildtype) and target (crp KO) show statistically indistinguishable lag durations (|t|<2, or mean difference <15 min), AND no simultaneous utilization in crp KO (EX_glc__D_e and EX_ac_e never both high), observed in ≥4/5 seeds for each design. This would support the null hypothesis that catabolite repression is not the mechanism causing the lag and sequential utilization.

rivals.claim: Nutrient-quality sensing: cells co-utilize both carbon sources but dynamically allocate proteome to maximize growth rate, leading to glucose preference purely by kinetic/thermodynamic advantage (higher ATP yield, faster flux) without transcriptional repression of acetate genes. The lag reflects metabolic reprogramming (enzyme turnover, metabolite pool adjustment) independent of Crp-cAMP.
rivals.distinguishing_result: The crp gene_knockout would show the same diauxic phenotype as wildtype: lag duration ≥20 min and sequential fluxes (EX_glc__D_e >10 then EX_ac_e >5, non-overlapping), because glucose's kinetic advantage persists even without catabolite repression. Welch t-test on lag durations (wildtype vs crp-KO) would yield |t|<2 (not significant). If instead the crp-KO shows lag <10 min or simultaneous fluxes, this rival is refuted and H1 (catabolite repression) is supported.

rivals.claim: Metabolic toxicity: acetate accumulation or overflow metabolism during glucose consumption inhibits growth; the lag reflects detoxification/adaptation (e.g., acetate assimilation pathway activation), not enzyme induction driven by catabolite repression.
rivals.distinguishing_result: A timeline with glucose alone (no acetate added initially) followed by natural glucose depletion would show negligible lag (<5 min) when cells transition to endogenous acetate (secreted during glucose overflow) or to stationary phase, because no exogenous acetate toxicity occurred. If candidate_design #4 (timeline 0 minimal+glucose(10mM), no acetate) shows immediate stationary phase or <5 min lag upon glucose exhaustion, and candidate_design #1 (glucose+acetate) shows ≥20 min lag, the difference implicates acetate-specific induction (supporting H1) rather than generic glucose-exhaustion adaptation. If both show similar ≥20 min lags, toxicity/adaptation (not catabolite repression) is supported. Additionally, crp-KO abolishing the lag (as predicted by H1) would refute the toxicity rival, because detoxification is Crp-independent.

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator faithfully represents catabolite repression (Crp-cAMP regulation of acetate-utilization operons such as acs, pta-ackA, and the glyoxylate shunt) through its FBA regulatory constraints or transcriptional network, such that glucose presence and Crp function prevent acetate uptake even when acetate is available. The model does not expose mRNA or cAMP concentration channels, but the exchange-flux pattern (sequential utilization) and the crp-knockout phenotype (loss of repression) are the operational signatures of this regulatory logic.

Initial concentrations of glucose (10 mM) and acetate (20 mM) are within the physiological range and sufficient to observe depletion and transition within ~10 generations (~6–8 hours at typical growth rates).

The timeline perturbation allows specification of initial mixed-carbon medium (glucose + acetate); glucose will deplete naturally mid-run due to preferential consumption. A mid-run carbon-source switch (adding a new sugar) is not permitted by the envelope, so we rely on natural depletion of the preferred carbon source.

The simulator outputs growth_rate and exchange fluxes (EX_glc__D_e, EX_ac_e) at timestep resolution ≤5 min, sufficient to detect a 20–60 min lag phase and to segment the time-series into exponential phases. If temporal resolution is coarser, the lag may be underestimated or missed.

Growth-rate segmentation (identifying lag phases by local minima and exponential fits) can be reliably automated from the time-series with signal-to-noise sufficient to distinguish genuine phase transitions from stochastic fluctuations; manual inspection may be needed if noise obscures boundaries.

The model's FBA or kinetic module computes exchange fluxes (EX_glc__D_e, EX_ac_e) that reflect actual uptake rates, not just internal reaction fluxes. Negative exchange flux indicates secretion; positive (or sign convention may vary) indicates uptake. We assume the convention is clear from model documentation.
Stochastic variation across seeds reflects intrinsic noise (gene expression, cell-cycle variability), not extrinsic batch-to-batch differences in medium composition.

SCOPE RESTRICTION (addressing 'sugar' vs 'carbon source'): The user's question asks 'How do bacteria choose between two sugars?' The classical diauxie experiments (Monod 1949) used glucose and a second carbon source; the textbook example in E. coli is often glucose–lactose or glucose–arabinose. However, the current simulator envelope does not allow a mid-run switch between two sugars (e.g., we cannot add lactose after glucose depletion). The timeline perturbation permits specifying an initial mixture, but the available static carbon sources (acetate, fumarate, malate, succinate) are organic acids, not sugars. Acetate is used here because: (a) it is the primary overflow metabolite of glucose and a well-characterized secondary carbon source in E. coli, (b) catabolite repression of acetate utilization (via Crp-cAMP regulation of acs and aceBAK) is extensively documented, and (c) glucose–acetate diauxie is a widely studied proxy for the general phenomenon. We acknowledge that acetate is not a sugar; the hypothesis tests the catabolite-repression mechanism of carbon-source hierarchical utilization, which applies to sugars (lactose, arabinose) and non-sugar carbon sources (acetate, glycerol) alike. The claim is revised to say 'carbon sources' where mechanistic accuracy requires it, but interprets the user's question as asking about the general mechanism of nutrient hierarchy, for which glucose–acetate is a valid and instrumentally feasible instance.

The crp gene encodes the catabolite repressor protein (Crp, also called CAP). Its knockout is simulated by the model as loss of Crp-dependent transcriptional activation of catabolite-repressed operons. We assume the simulator's gene_knockout perturbation for crp faithfully represents this regulatory defect. If the model does not encode Crp regulation (i.e., crp knockout has no phenotype), the test will fail, and this will be evident in the results (no difference between designs #1 and #3). This is an auxiliary assumption about simulator fidelity.

GENERALIZATION CAVEAT: This hypothesis tests the canonical diauxie mechanism in E. coli with the glucose–acetate pair. The user's question 'How do bacteria choose between two sugars?' is general, but testing a distribution claim across multiple species or sugar pairs is beyond the current instrument envelope (only E. coli whole-cell model available; limited sugar-shift options in timeline). We answer the question for the E. coli / glucose–acetate case and acknowledge that generalization to other bacteria (Bacillus, Pseudomonas) or other sugar pairs (lactose/arabinose, glucose/xylose) would require additional models and designs. The catabolite-repression mechanism is widely conserved in Gram-negative bacteria, so this instance is representative of the broader phenomenon, but the hypothesis does not claim universality across all bacteria or all sugar pairs.

claude.rationale:
Q1 (falsifiable): The falsifier names multiple concrete refuting results that are genuine risky prohibitions — e.g., wildtype growth remaining monotonic (trough >60%), lag <20%, co-utilization (acetate rising while glucose >20% of peak), R<3.0 (passive dilution), or crp_ko showing R≥3.0. Any of these would refute H1. PASS.

Q2 (operationalized): Every construct is bound to a named measurable observable (diauxic_growth→growth_rate, glucose_preference→exchange_flux[EX_glc__D_e], active_catabolite_repression→protein[CPLX-ACONTA]/dry_mass, acetate_utilization_onset→exchange_flux[EX_ac_e]). The falsifier names a statistical test (Welch t-test, |t|≥2.0) and numeric thresholds (trough ≤0.60, lag ≥20%, R≥3.0). PASS.

Q3 (discriminating): Two rivals named (acetate toxicity, passive enzyme dilution), each with distinguishing experiments — crp_ko and actP_ko for toxicity; the R ratio (aconitase/dry_mass fold-change ≤1.2 vs ≥3.0) and crp_ko R behavior for dilution. Candidate designs include the crp and actP knockouts. PASS.

Q4 (quantitative): predicted_effect gives direction and numeric magnitudes — peak ~0.9/h, trough ≤0.5/h (≤56%), lag ≥20% runtime, R≥3.0 (wt) vs ≤1.5 (crp_ko), lag fold-change ≥2.5. PASS.

Q5 (specified): IV (crp/actP knockout vs wildtype, media condition), DV (growth_rate, fluxes, aconitase/dry_mass), and predicted directions all present. PASS.

Q6 (consistent): H1, H0, falsifier, and rival predictions are mutually coherent; thresholds align across sections (trough 60%, lag 20%, R 3.0). The scope limitation about overflow-acetate proxy is honestly flagged but does not create internal contradiction. PASS.

gpt.rationale: The hypothesis is well-formed in terms of methodological quality. It is falsifiable as it specifies concrete results that would refute the hypothesis, such as the absence of a biphasic growth pattern or the presence of co-utilization of sugars. It is operationalized with clear definitions of constructs and measurable observables, along with statistical tests and numeric thresholds for falsification. The hypothesis is discriminating, as it includes rival hypotheses with distinguishing experiments that separate them from the main hypothesis. It is quantitative, providing specific numeric predictions for growth rates, lag durations, and enzyme abundance changes. The hypothesis is well-specified, with clear independent and dependent variables and predicted directions of effects. Finally, it is internally consistent, with no contradictions between the hypothesis, falsifier, and rival predictions.

hypothesis.question: How do bacteria choose between two sugars in the same flask?
hypothesis.claim: In a mixed-sugar environment (glucose + acetate co-present from the start), E. coli exhibits diauxic growth: the preferred sugar (glucose) is consumed first while catabolite repression (CRP-cAMP) suppresses acetate catabolic gene expression; acetate utilization begins only after glucose depletion triggers a lag phase during which acetate-utilization enzymes are induced, producing a biphasic growth curve. The lag and enzyme induction are driven by active transcriptional repression (not passive dilution), evidenced by a sharp rise in the aconitase/dry_mass ratio after glucose exhaustion.
hypothesis.h1: When glucose and acetate are co-present, CRP-cAMP–mediated catabolite repression actively suppresses acetate catabolic genes (acs, actP, TCA enzymes) during the glucose phase, keeping their mass-specific abundance (protein/dry_mass ratio) low; acetate uptake and growth on acetate commence only after glucose is exhausted and a lag (≥20% of total runtime) during which acetate enzymes are induced and their mass-specific abundance rises ≥3-fold, yielding a bimodal growth_rate time-series.
hypothesis.h0: When glucose and acetate are co-present, both sugars are co-utilized from the start with no catabolite repression; growth_rate follows a smooth single exponential with no lag phase (lag <5% of runtime), acetate uptake begins while glucose flux remains >20% of its peak, and acetate catabolic enzyme abundance (aconitase/dry_mass) remains constant (±20%) throughout, indicating passive dilution rather than active repression.

operational_defs.measure: Biphasic time-series: growth_rate exhibits two exponential segments separated by a trough (minimum growth_rate ≤60% of first-phase peak), vs. monotonic single-phase kinetics in the null. Lag duration (time from glucose depletion to recovery of growth_rate to ≥90% of second-phase steady-state) is ≥20% of total simulation time.
Glucose exchange flux (import, negative sign) falls to ≤5% of its peak magnitude before acetate uptake flux reaches ≥50% of its maximum, indicating sequential substrate depletion.

Mass-specific aconitase abundance (protein[CPLX-ACONTA]/dry_mass) remains ≤30% of its post-shift steady-state value during the glucose phase (while glucose flux >10% of peak), then rises ≥3-fold after glucose depletion, indicating active transcriptional repression that is relieved upon glucose exhaustion (not passive dilution, which would maintain constant protein/dry_mass).
Acetate uptake flux (import, negative magnitude) remains ≤15% of its maximum while glucose flux >10% of peak, then rises to ≥80% of maximum after glucose exhaustion.

hypothesis.predicted_effect: In a basal condition with both glucose and acetate available from t=0 (approximated by starting in basal minimal media, which contains glucose, and observing endogenous acetate dynamics from overflow metabolism), wildtype exhibits a bimodal growth_rate time-series: first-phase peak ~0.9/h, trough ≤0.5/h (≤56% of peak), second-phase recovery to ~0.6/h, with lag duration ≥20% of runtime. Mass-specific aconitase (protein[CPLX-ACONTA]/dry_mass) rises ≥3-fold from glucose phase to acetate phase. In a crp gene_knockout (abolishing catabolite repression), the trough depth is reduced to ≥70% of first peak (≤30% drop), lag duration is ≤8% of runtime, and aconitase/dry_mass remains relatively flat (varies <50% across the run, indicating constitutive expression). Fold-change in lag duration: wildtype_lag / crp_ko_lag ≥2.5. Fold-change in aconitase/dry_mass ratio (acetate phase / glucose phase): wildtype ≥3.0, crp_ko ≤1.5.

falsifier.decision_rule: For each replicate, identify the minimum growth_rate (trough) and measure lag duration as the time interval from the trough to recovery (growth_rate ≥90% of second-phase mean over final 20% of runtime). Compute mean trough depth (min_growth_rate / first_peak_growth_rate) and mean lag duration (as % of total runtime) across seeds. Perform Welch t-test on lag duration (wildtype vs. crp_ko). ADDITIONALLY, for each replicate compute the mass-specific aconitase ratio R = (protein[CPLX-ACONTA]/dry_mass)_acetate_phase / (protein[CPLX-ACONTA]/dry_mass)_glucose_phase, where glucose_phase is defined as the first 40% of runtime and acetate_phase as the final 20%. Compute mean R across seeds for wildtype and crp_ko. Reject H0 if ALL of the following hold: (1) wildtype mean trough depth ≤0.60 AND mean lag ≥20% of runtime, AND (2) crp_ko mean trough depth ≥0.70 OR mean lag ≤8% of runtime, AND (3) Welch |t| ≥2.0 for lag duration difference, AND (4) wildtype mean R ≥3.0 (active repression then induction) while crp_ko mean R ≤1.5 (constitutive, no repression). Additionally, reject H0 if acetate flux (exchange_flux[EX_ac_e]) in wildtype reaches ≥50% of its maximum while glucose flux (exchange_flux[EX_glc__D_e]) remains >20% of peak (simultaneous co-utilization).
falsifier.refuting_result: Wildtype growth_rate remains monotonic (trough depth >60% of first peak) or lag <20% of runtime, OR crp_ko shows the same biphasic pattern as wildtype (trough ≤60%, lag ≥18%), OR acetate uptake in wildtype begins while glucose flux >20% of peak (co-utilization), OR wildtype mass-specific aconitase ratio R <3.0 (indicating passive dilution rather than active repression), OR crp_ko shows R ≥3.0 (repression persists even without CRP, falsifying the CRP-dependence claim).

rivals.claim: Rival 1 (Acetate toxicity): Acetate is toxic (acidifies cytoplasm) during rapid glucose growth; cells delay acetate uptake to avoid acid stress, not because of CRP-mediated catabolite repression. The lag reflects detoxification/adaptation, not enzyme induction.
rivals.distinguishing_result: A crp gene_knockout (abolishing catabolite repression) still shows the same lag duration (≥18% of runtime) and trough depth (≤60%) as wildtype, because toxicity (not transcriptional repression) drives the delay. An independent toxicity control—gene_knockout of actP (acetate transporter), which prevents acetate uptake entirely—shows similar first-phase growth_rate as wildtype (no toxicity when acetate is excluded), confirming that any wildtype lag is due to acetate presence. If H1 (catabolite repression) is true, crp_ko exhibits sharply reduced lag (≤8%) and shallow trough (≥70%), AND actP_ko shows monotonic growth on glucose alone with no second phase (no acetate uptake), distinguishing repression from toxicity.

rivals.claim: Rival 2 (Passive enzyme dilution): The lag reflects passive dilution of acetate-catabolic enzymes by rapid glucose-phase growth, not active transcriptional repression. Acetate enzymes are expressed constitutively at low level; during fast glucose growth they are diluted below functional threshold, then accumulate by cessation of dilution (growth slowdown) when glucose runs out.
rivals.distinguishing_result: Protein[CPLX-ACONTA] concentration tracks dry_mass with constant mass-specific abundance (aconitase/dry_mass = constant ±15%) throughout the glucose phase, indicating no active repression—just dilution by growth. The mass-specific aconitase ratio R (acetate_phase / glucose_phase) is ≤1.2 in wildtype (no induction, just passive re-concentration when growth slows). If H1 (active repression) is true, wildtype shows R ≥3.0 (aconitase/dry_mass rises sharply after glucose depletion, decoupled from passive growth dilution). Additionally, in crp_ko, R remains ≤1.5 (flat mass-specific abundance across the run, confirming constitutive expression when repression is lifted), whereas if passive dilution were the mechanism, crp_ko would also show R ≥3.0 (same dilution dynamics as wildtype).

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator faithfully models CRP-cAMP–mediated catabolite repression and the induction kinetics of acetate-utilization operons (acs, actP, TCA-cycle genes including CPLX-ACONTA).

Basal minimal media dynamics (with glucose as primary carbon source) produce endogenous acetate via overflow metabolism during rapid growth, which is then re-consumed after glucose depletion, thereby approximating a mixed-sugar environment. (The envelope does not support explicit dual-carbon media from t=0; we rely on this overflow→re-consumption cycle as a natural diauxie proxy.)

Stochastic noise across seeds (intrinsic variability in lag timing, trough depth, aconitase/dry_mass ratio) is smaller than the predicted effect size: replicate CV for lag duration ≤25%, for trough depth ≤15%, and for mass-specific aconitase ratio R ≤30%.

Aconitase A (protein[CPLX-ACONTA]) abundance is a valid proxy for acetate catabolic capacity; other TCA enzymes (citrate synthase, isocitrate dehydrogenase) show coordinated regulation and similar repression/induction kinetics.

The model's flux-balance analysis (FBA) correctly predicts substrate preference hierarchy (glucose > acetate) based on thermodynamic and regulatory constraints, and does not artificially enforce diauxie by hard-coded substrate-exclusion logic.

Any acetate toxicity effect (cytoplasmic acidification, growth inhibition) is captured in the model's physiology (e.g., via ATP cost of proton export or feedback on metabolic fluxes). The actP knockout isolates the toxicity mechanism by preventing acetate import; if the model does not simulate pH/toxicity explicitly, this control may not fully discriminate toxicity from repression, but it remains the best available in-envelope test.

The absence of a true simultaneous dual-carbon condition (e.g., glucose + acetate both supplied exogenously from t=0) is a scope limitation; however, the overflow-acetate dynamics in basal minimal media are biologically realistic and sufficient to test the core claim of catabolite repression and sequential utilization.

SCOPE LIMITATION: This hypothesis tests the glucose–acetate pair and CRP-cAMP catabolite repression specifically, not the general question 'how bacteria choose between two sugars.' The mechanism (CRP-cAMP) is known to govern many sugar hierarchies (glucose > lactose, glucose > arabinose, etc.), but other regulatory systems (PTS-mediated inducer exclusion, cAMP-independent repression, post-translational control) also contribute. A complete answer to the user's question would require testing multiple sugar pairs and regulatory knockouts; here we focus on the canonical diauxie case (Monod 1949) as the primary exemplar of hierarchical sugar choice. If this test confirms CRP-cAMP–mediated repression for glucose–acetate, the mechanism generalizes to other CRP-regulated sugars; if it fails, alternative mechanisms must be tested.

claude.rationale:
Q1 (falsifiable): The falsifier names concrete refuting results — unimodal distribution (dip p≥0.05), OR lower-mode mean >0.15 1/h, OR lower-mode fraction <20%. These are risky prohibitions that could genuinely fail (the population might simply be unimodal). PASS.

Q2 (operationalized): Every construct is bound to a named measurable observable — slow-growth subpopulation→growth_rate (dip test + GMM), stringent response→ppgpp_conc, physiology→ribosome_conc, heterogeneity→CV of growth_rate. The falsifier names a statistical test (Hartigan dip test) and numeric thresholds (p<0.05, ≤0.15 1/h, 20% fraction). PASS.

Q3 (discriminating): Three rivals are named (deterministic nutrient depletion, ppGpp-independent TA modules, ppGpp-independent bistability), each with distinguishing quantitative results tied to specific designs (relA knockout, ppGpp clamp) that separate them from H1. PASS.

Q4 (quantitative): predicted_effect states direction and numeric magnitude — bimodal with lower mode ≤0.15 1/h, ≥20% fraction, ppGpp increases fraction by ≥15 pp, CV ≥0.35, relA knockout reduces to ≤10%. PASS.

Q5 (specified): IV (ppGpp clamp 2×, relA knockout, wildtype), DV (growth_rate distribution, ribosome_conc), and predicted direction (increase/decrease in slow-growth fraction) are all present. PASS.

Q6 (consistent): H1, H0, falsifier, and rival predictions are mutually consistent; thresholds align across sections (e.g., ≥15 pp increase for ppGpp, ≤10% for relA knockout) with no contradictions. PASS.

gpt.rationale: The hypothesis is well-formed in terms of methodological quality. It is falsifiable because it specifies concrete conditions under which the hypothesis would be refuted, such as unimodal growth rate distributions or insufficient slow-growth mode fraction. It is operationalized with clear definitions of constructs and measurable observables, including statistical tests and numeric thresholds. The hypothesis is discriminating, as it presents rival hypotheses with distinguishing experiments. It is quantitative, providing specific numeric predictions for growth rate distributions and changes in slow-growth mode fraction. The hypothesis is well-specified, with clear independent and dependent variables and predicted directions of effect. Finally, it is internally consistent, with no contradictions between the hypothesis, falsifier, and rival predictions.

hypothesis.question: Why do some bacteria survive antibiotics without becoming resistant?
hypothesis.claim: A subpopulation of bacteria enters a slow-growth, low-ribosome persister-like state without genetic resistance mutations, and this fraction increases when the stringent response is activated. Although the simulator cannot model antibiotic exposure directly, the hypothesis tests whether ppGpp-regulated phenotypic heterogeneity in growth rate produces a distinct slow-growth subpopulation that would, in vivo, exhibit antibiotic tolerance.
hypothesis.h1: Phenotypic heterogeneity in growth rate, regulated by the ppGpp-mediated stringent response, produces a distinct slow-growth subpopulation: wildtype/basal populations show bimodal growth_rate distributions with a slow-growth mode centered ≤0.15 1/h, and elevating ppGpp increases the fraction of cells in this mode by ≥15 percentage points relative to wildtype.
hypothesis.h0: Clonal populations exhibit unimodal growth_rate distributions with low variance; no distinct slow-growth subpopulation emerges, and ppGpp elevation shifts the mean uniformly without increasing a slow-growth fraction.

operational_defs.measure: Bimodality of growth_rate distribution across ≥50 seeds at generation 10 (Hartigan dip test p<0.05), with the lower mode mean ≤0.15 1/h. Fraction of seeds assigned to the lower mode by Gaussian mixture model (2 components) fitting.
ppGpp concentration (uM) clamped to 2× basal via ppgpp_conc perturbation
active ribosome concentration (uM) at generation 10, expected to anti-correlate with ppGpp elevation
coefficient of variation (CV) of growth_rate across ≥50 seeds at generation 10

hypothesis.predicted_effect: Wildtype/basal with 50 seeds will show bimodal growth_rate distribution (dip test p<0.05) with lower mode mean ≤0.15 1/h and ≥20% of seeds assigned to this mode by GMM. Elevating ppGpp (ppgpp_conc clamp to 2×) will increase the slow-growth mode fraction by ≥15 percentage points (to ≥35%). The CV of growth_rate across seeds will be ≥0.35 for wildtype/basal. Knocking out relA will reduce the slow-growth fraction to ≤10% (a ≥10 percentage point drop vs wildtype), confirming ppGpp-dependence.

falsifier.decision_rule: Hartigan dip test for bimodality on growth_rate distribution across 50 seeds at generation 10; reject unimodal H0 if p<0.05. Fit 2-component Gaussian mixture model (GMM); reject H1 if (a) dip test p≥0.05 (unimodal), OR (b) the lower-mode mean >0.15 1/h, OR (c) the lower-mode fraction <20%.
falsifier.refuting_result: Unimodal growth_rate distribution (dip test p≥0.05), OR lower-mode mean >0.15 1/h, OR lower-mode fraction <20% would refute the claim that phenotypic heterogeneity produces a distinct slow-growth subpopulation.

rivals.claim: Slow growth is purely a deterministic consequence of nutrient depletion or metabolic steady-state convergence, not stochastic phenotypic heterogeneity—all seeds converge to the same low growth rate by generation 10.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If this rival is true, growth_rate variance across 50 seeds will be low (CV <0.15) and unimodal (dip test p≥0.05), with all seeds clustered near the same value. The ppgpp_conc clamp to 2× would shift the mean uniformly without increasing bimodality or the slow-growth mode fraction by ≥15 percentage points.

rivals.claim: Persistence is independent of the stringent response and ppGpp; it arises from toxin-antitoxin modules or other ppGpp-independent stochastic switches.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If this rival is true, the gene_knockout/basal/relA design will show a slow-growth mode fraction ≥15% (not significantly lower than wildtype/basal; difference <5 percentage points), and the ppgpp_conc clamp to 2× will NOT increase the slow-growth fraction by ≥15 percentage points (difference <10 percentage points vs wildtype).

rivals.claim: Bimodality arises from bistability in a ppGpp-independent regulatory circuit (e.g., a positive-feedback loop in central metabolism), such that ppGpp elevation shifts both modes uniformly rather than recruiting cells into the slow-growth mode.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If this rival is true, ppgpp_conc clamp to 2× will preserve bimodality (dip test p<0.05) but shift both mode means downward by similar amounts, leaving the slow-growth mode fraction unchanged (<5 percentage point increase). The relA knockout will also retain bimodality with a slow-growth fraction ≥15%.

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator's stochastic engine produces biologically realistic phenotypic heterogeneity across isogenic seeds (intrinsic noise in gene expression and partitioning at division).

The slow-growth subpopulation identified by bimodality and GMM mode assignment operationally corresponds to the persister-like state that, in vivo, confers antibiotic tolerance. The simulator does not model antibiotic exposure or killing; this hypothesis tests the upstream phenotypic heterogeneity mechanism only.
The basal condition does not impose severe nutrient stress that would deterministically drive all cells to slow growth by generation 10, allowing stochastic heterogeneity to manifest.
RelA is the dominant ppGpp synthetase under basal conditions; its knockout substantially reduces ppGpp concentration and stringent-response signaling.
The model's ribosome allocation and ppGpp feedback are mechanistically coupled such that elevated ppGpp reduces ribosome concentration and growth rate.
A 2-component Gaussian mixture model is sufficient to capture the bimodal structure if present; more complex multi-modal distributions are not expected under basal conditions.
Generation 10 provides sufficient time for phenotypic heterogeneity to develop and stabilize without deterministic nutrient depletion dominating all replicates.

claude.rationale:
q1 (Falsifiable): PASS. The falsifier names concrete refuting results — WT CV ≤0.20 or slow-growth fraction <8% or indistinguishable from relA+spoT knockout (|t|<2). This is a risky prohibition; H1 predicts CV≥0.30 and could genuinely fail if the population is unimodal.

q2 (Operationalized): PASS. Every construct is bound to a named measurable observable (persister→growth_rate, stringent response→ppgpp_conc, ribosome allocation→ribosome_conc, heterogeneity→CV of growth_rate, nutrient stress→fraction_trna_charged). The falsifier names a statistical test (Welch t-test on CV) and numeric thresholds (|t|<2, CV<0.25, fraction<8%).

q3 (Discriminating): PASS. Three named rivals (deterministic nutrient depletion, division-noise, pre-stress heterogeneity unrelated to survival), each with distinguishing quantitative predictions (e.g., Rival 1: CV≤0.15 + Spearman |ρ|>0.8 with fraction_trna_charged; Rival 2: knockout retains CV≥0.25). The candidate designs (relA/spoT knockout, ppGpp clamp, rrna knockout) provide the perturbations to separate them.

q4 (Quantitative): PASS. predicted_effect gives direction and numeric magnitude: CV≥0.30, ≥10% seeds <50% median, ppgpp >1.5× median; knockout collapses to CV≤0.15 and tail ≤3%; clamp reduces median growth ≥40% with CV≤0.20.

q5 (Specified): PASS. IV (perturbations: relA/spoT knockout, ppGpp 2.0× clamp, rrna knockout), DV (growth_rate CV, ppgpp_conc, ribosome_conc), and predicted directions all present.

q6 (Consistent): PASS. H1 (ppGpp-driven stochastic heterogeneity → knockout collapses CV) is consistent with the falsifier (refute if indistinguishable from knockout) and with rivals (division-noise predicts knockout retains CV — the direct negation). No internal contradictions; the proxy assumptions are explicitly flagged rather than smuggled in.
